title: Rootless podman and authbind
body:
**BUG REPORT**

/kind bug

**Description**
Hello all, podman rootless container wont bind to port 80 with authbind configured while it works with another commands like python3 -m http.server 80
I dont want use ip_unprivileged_port_start (I just need to exposed few ports) to or firewall rules (I use firewalld and local processes dont follow firewalld port forwarding without adding iptables/nft rules in parrallel, no process attached to port with netstat)
Is it podman internals which refuse to bind before trying it or some podman specificity ?

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**
1. ```touch /etc/authbind/byport/80```
2. ```chown debian /etc/authbind/byport/80```
3. ```chmod 700 /etc/authbind/byport/80```
4. ```authbind podman run -d -p 80:80 docker.io/traefik/whoami```
or
5. ```authbind --deep podman run -d -p 80:80 docker.io/traefik/whoami```

**Describe the results you received:**
```Error: rootlessport cannot expose privileged port 80, you can add 'net.ipv4.ip_unprivileged_port_start=80' to /etc/sysctl.conf (currently 1024), or choose a larger port number (>= 1024): listen tcp 0.0.0.0:80: bind: permission denied```

**Describe the results you expected:**
run this container on port 80 as expected

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**
It works well with others softwares like python's http.server
```authbind python3 -m http.server 80```

**Output of `podman version`:**
Debian 11 podman by apt
```

API Version:  3.0.0
Go Version:   go1.15.9
Built:        Thu Jan  1 01:00:00 1970

```

comments.body:
From http://manpages.ubuntu.com/manpages/trusty/man1/authbind.1.html
> if  the  program  doesn't  already  have  an  effective uid of 0

Rootless podman will always run in a userns namespace with your user mapped to root, so the euid is always 0.
Also since podman runs in the user namespace the setuid authbind program will not work correctly because the hosts root is not mapped into this namespace.

I don't think authbind can work correctly with "newer" kernel features. It looks to me that authbind was written before namespaces and capabilities existed.

I think redirecting the ports via firewall is the best solution.
Podman does not use the rootlesskit binary. Podman 4.0 added a rootlessport binary to /usr/libexec/podman/rootlessport where this might work but I do not know.

Ahh rootlessport, I thought that seemed familiar, so I figured I'd ask.
I might give this a try in a dev environment.
Adding a File Capability requires approval of the distributions. We would need to get multiple distributions to agree to allow a new program with file caps to be installed, if we did this in Podman.
@rhatdan I would think that this would be best left up to the server administrator. I'm sure they wouldn't want just any user to be able to bind to any low level port.
Sure if you want to document how to do this, I am fine with that.  If it happens by default, is where I have a problem.
Yeah, I would not want that to happen by default in a rootless environment.

For anyone that finds this in the future, I could not get this to work. With my research, it is because rootlessport is executed in a user namespace.

`sudo setcap cap_net_bind_service=+ep /usr/libexec/podman/rootlessport`
